title: Soy Sauce on Cotton
stainChemistry: Soy sauce contains caramel color compounds (melanoidins), salt, amino acids, and tannin-like phenolics from fermentation. On cotton, the melanoidins bond to fiber through a combination of hydrogen bonding and Maillard-type crosslinks. The high salt content acts as a mordant, fixing the brown color deeper into the fiber matrix.
whyThisWorks: Flushing with cold water immediately removes salt and loose pigment from cotton. The tannin spotter breaks melanoidin-fiber bonds. An oxidizer addresses the persistent brown color. Pre-rinsing is unusually important for soy sauce — the salt component acts as a dye fixative, so removing it quickly prevents the stain from becoming permanent.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color

homeSolutions.agent: Rinse within 60 seconds — salt is the enemy
homeSolutions.instruction: Soy sauce is a tannin stain with a lot of salt. Salt acts as a mordant — it fixes the brown color DEEPER into the fabric the longer it sits. If you can reach a sink: hold the garment under cold tap water, stain-side DOWN, for 30–60 seconds immediately. That single step takes a 30-minute treatment down to 5.

On an inside seam, dab a drop of Dawn + 3% peroxide solution. Blot with a white cloth after two minutes. If dye transfers, stop and wash cold. If clean, proceed. Tannin rule: no alkaline cleaners (ammonia, baking soda, untreated OxiClean) — they lock the brown color into the fiber permanently.

Press a white cloth or paper-towel stack straight down on the stain. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work outside in. Rubbing cotton when wet breaks down the fiber AND spreads the melanoidin pigment — you'll turn a quarter-sized spot into a palm-sized one.

Turn the garment inside out and run cold water through the BACK of the stain for 30–60 seconds. This pushes the soy sauce out the way it came in, and — critical for soy — flushes the salt out before it finishes locking the stain. Skip hot water: heat polymerizes the melanoidins (browning compounds) permanently.

Mix one tablespoon of Dawn dish soap with one cup of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a small bowl. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, and dab the stain. Let it sit 10 minutes. Peroxide oxidizes the melanoidin browning compounds; Dawn lifts them. Blot with a clean damp cloth. Repeat up to two more times for older stains.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia or bleach. On brightly colored cotton, the step-2 patch test is your safety net.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar with one cup of cold water. Dab onto the area. The mild acid neutralizes peroxide residue and keeps any remaining tannin in its lighter form rather than drifting toward brown on drying. Blot dry.

Launder on cold with your normal detergent. DO NOT tumble-dry until you've inspected in daylight. Hang or lay flat; check for shadow in natural light. If any remains, repeat steps 5–6 before using dryer heat. Heat on a residual soy stain locks the remaining brown in.

If a pale tan shadow persists on white cotton, lay the damp garment flat in direct sunlight for 1–3 hours. UV naturally bleaches residual melanoidins without chemistry. Skip on colored cotton — sun fades dye.

If the stain survived two home rounds, was set by a dryer, or is older than 48 hours, take it in. Tell them: "Soy sauce on cotton, treated at home with Dawn + 3% peroxide, vinegar rinse. No heat, no alkaline products used. Residual tan halo remains." That tells them to go straight to tannin formula + 6% peroxide rather than guessing at pigment type.

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
fiberSensitivities: Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide

escalation.when: If soy sauce stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is soy sauce on cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
